title: ASIC warns banks over return to wealth market
full_text:
Australia’s corporate regulator has urged banks not to make the same mistakes as they return to the lucrative wealth market, and took a swipe at lenders for failing to get even the basics right.
At the annual Australian Banking Association conference, which had as its theme “Banking boldly”, the Australian Securities & Investments Commission’s new chair cautioned lenders to remember the lessons from the financial services royal commission and to not be distracted by bold innovation.
“There’s no point in being bold and creative if you’re bungling the basics. How frequently the basics are gotten wrong,” Sarah Court said.
“We’re very rarely in court dealing with some bold new innovation; what we’re in court about is when the bank hasn’t paid the interest rates it said it would, or hasn’t responded to hardship. That’s where the trust risk lies for the sector, continuing to not get the basics right.” Ms Court, previously ASIC’s deputy chair, stepped into the top job on June 1. “From where I sit, it is very important that the lessons from the royal commission are not forgotten or diluted,” she said. “We’re conscious of the discussion to move back towards providing advice and… we’ll have some things to say about that.” Almost a decade later, major bank CEOs have been laying the groundwork for a potential return to the wealth market.
At the same conference on Wednesday, Westpac chief executive Anthony Miller said regulatory settings were holding banks back from providing even basic advice to customers.
He suggested banks could play a role in advice so customers were not caught out by scams and fraud. “So many people don’t have anyone to talk to on a commonsense financial advice path as it sits here today,” he said. “They see something on Facebook, or another social media platform, and they say ‘Oh I’ll invest in that’. It’s horrendous.” He dismissed the idea a sector move back into wealth was about getting fees or commissions.
